OF SRI LANKA

terms of Article 140 of the Constitution for
mandates in the nature of Writs of Certiorari,
Prohibition and Mandamus.
Herath Abeykoon Bandaralage Sanjeewa

Prabashini Jayasekara, S.C., for the
Respondents.
Supported on : 25.11.2025
Written Submissions
of the Petitioner
tendered on : 18.12.2025
Written Submissions
of the Respondents
tendered on : 12.02.2026.
Decided on : 27.02.2026.
K. M. S. DISSANAYAKE, J.
The Petitioner in the instant application seeks inter-alia, a mandate in the nature
of a writ of certiorari to quash the decision taken to interdict him without pay as
contained in the letter dated 08.09.2025 issued by the 2nd Respondent marked
‘P11’; a mandate in the nature of a writ of certiorari to quash the decisions
contained in the letter dated 08.09.2025 issued by the 2nd Respondent marked
‘P11’; a mandate in the nature of a writ of certiorari to quash the decisions of the
Board of Directors of the 1st Respondent taken at the meeting bearing No. 336
held on 29.08.2025 communicated by the letter dated 08.09.2025 marked ‘P11’;
a mandate in the nature of a writ of certiorari to quash the decision contained in
the letter dated 01.09.2025 issued by the 2nd Respondent marked ‘P10’; a
mandate in the nature of a writ of prohibition preventing the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th
, 6th , 7th , 8th , 9th, 10th, and/or 11th Respondents and/or any one or more of
them and/or their servants and agents, and/or their successors in office from
taking any steps to charge the Petitioner and/or conduct a disciplinary inquiry
Page 5 of 24
against the Petitioner based on the decision of the Board of Directors of the 1st
Respondent taken at the Board Meeting No: 336 dated 29.8.2025 and/or the
report of the audit conducted by the Auditor General’s Office during the month
of May 2025; a mandate in the nature of a writ of mandamus directing the 1st,
2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, 8th, 9th, 10th, and/or 11th Respondents and/or any one
or more of them and/or their servants and agents, and/or their successors in
office to pay the salary and emoluments of the Petitioner; a mandate in the
nature of a writ of mandamus directing the 1st , 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th , 8th , 9th,
10th and/or 11th Respondents and/or any one or more of them and/or their
servants and agents, and/or their successors in office to pay the Petitioner
backwages and emoluments the Petitioner is lawfully entitled to; and an array of
interim orders as prayed for in prayer ‘k’ to ‘o’ of his petition dated 08.10.2025.
When this matter came on before us for support of the application for notice as
well as the application for interim orders, learned State Counsel raised a
preliminary legal objection as to the maintainability of the instant application on
the premise that the decision taken to interdict the Petitioner without pay as
contained in the letter dated 08.09.2025 issued by the 2nd Respondent marked
‘P11’ had been made by the Respondents in the exercise of the powers derived
upon them by the contract of employment between the Petitioner and the 1st
Respondent (P2) and not in the exercise of any statutory powers and therefore,
the decision sought to be impugned by the Petitioner in the instant application
(P11) is not amenable to writ jurisdiction but to the private law and therefore,
the instant application should be dismissed in-limine.
We heard the learned Counsel for the Petitioner in support of both the
application for notice of the instant application as well as the application for
interim relief. We also, heard the learned State Counsel for the Respondents in
opposition to the application for notice as well as the interim relief and also in
support of the preliminary legal objection so raised by him as to the
Page 6 of 24
maintainability of the instant application. Hence, this matter comes up before
us today for order thereon.
It would I think, be appropriate in the first instance, to deal with the preliminary
legal objection so raised to Court by the learned State Counsel for the
Respondents as to the maintainability of the instant application as enumerated
above.
It may now, be examined.
In view of the preliminary objection so raised, the pivotal question that would
first, arise for our consideration at this juncture is, as to whether the decision
taken to interdict the Petitioner without pay as contained in the letter dated
08.09.2025 issued by the 2nd Respondent marked ‘P11’ had been made by the
Respondents in the exercise of any statutory powers derived upon them by any
statute; and if so, whether the decision impugned (P11) is amendable to the writ
jurisdiction of this Court as contended by the Petitioner, or; as to whether the
decision impugned (P11) had been made by the Respondents in the exercise of
the powers derived upon them by the contract of employment between the
Petitioner and the 1st Respondent (P2) and not in the exercise of any statutory
powers so derived upon them; and if so, whether the decision impugned (P11)
is amenable to the remedy under private law and not the remedy under public
law by way of mandates in the nature of writs as prayed for by the Petitioner; as
contended by the learned State Counsel for the Respondents.
It is in this context, let me now, precisely, ascertain what the Petitioner seeks to
impugn in the instant application?
As is discernible from a careful scrutiny of the averments contained in the
petition filed before this Court by the Petitioner, the documents annexed thereto,
and the submissions oral and written made by the learned Counsel for the
Petitioner before us in support of the application for notice of the instant
application and the interim orders, it is the decision taken by the Respondents
Page 7 of 24
to interdict the Petitioner without pay (P11) which the Petitioner now, seeks to
impugn in the instant application before us.
The question that would next, arise for our consideration is the pivotal basis
upon which the Petitioner seeks to impugn the decision(P11) before us?
As can be deducible from a careful scrutiny of the averments contained in the
petition filed before this Court by the Petitioner, the documents annexed thereto,
and the submissions oral and written made by the learned Counsel for the
Petitioner before us in support of the application for notice of the instant
application and the interim orders, the pivotal basis therefor being the alleged
unreasonable and irrational conduct of the Respondents to interdict the
Petitioner without pay, as communicated by letter dated 8.9.2025 (P11)
without any preliminary investigation/inquiry being held, and/or without
offering the Petitioner the opportunity to provide any explanation/show
cause which according to the Petitioner, is a clear breach of the
Establishment Code and rules of Natural Justice. [Emphasis is mine]
It was averred by the Petitioner in the averments in paragraph 4 (a) of the petition
that the 1st Respondent is a limited liability company incorporated on 23.7.1993
under the Companies Act No. 17 of 1982 as a fully government-owned company
and re-registered under the Companies Act No.7 of 2007 as a publicly owned
enterprise with state ownership, managing ten (10) Estates and that the 1st
Respondent was established by vesting lands managed by the State Plantations
Corporations in terms of the provisions of the Conversion of Corporations and
Government Owned Businesses Undertakings into Public Companies Act No. 23
of 1987 under the State Privatisation Policy and that the 1st Respondent operates
as a single shareholder company, with the Secretary to the Treasury being the
Golden Shareholder and that the notice in respect of the formation of the 1st
Respondent was published in the Extraordinary Gazette Notification No. 776/13
of July 1993 and that the State exercises all pervasive control over the 1st
Page 8 of 24
Respondent, and in view of the aforesaid, the Petitioner states that it is trite that
the 1st Respondent is an instrumentality of the State.
It is to be observed that, the Respondent in the submissions-oral or written, had
not in any manner, sought to deny or refute the assertion of the Petitioner that
the 1st Respondent is a State Agency.
A copy of the Board Resolution passed by the Board of Directors of Elkaduwa
Plantations Ltd, at the Board meeting of the Directors held on 29th August 2025
which was certified by the 1st Respondent as a true copy, had been tendered to
this Court by the Respondents by the motion dated 12.02.2026 with a copy to
the Petitioner. Hence, the Petitioner has been made fully, aware of the Board
Resolution and its contents thereby, giving him reasonable opportunity to
comment thereon, nevertheless, the Petitioner opted to remain silent without
making any comment on it. This Court is thus, entitled to consider the Board
Resolution and the contents therein by reason of the fact that a copy of the same
had duly, been tendered by the Respondents to the Petitioner in terms of the
motion filed before this Court by them on 12.02.2026
It is to be observed that it is this Board Resolution in which the decision taken
to interdict the Petitioner without pay as contained in the letter dated 08.09.2025
issued by the 2nd Respondent (P11), is contained.
It clearly, appears upon a careful perusal of the Board Resolution and the letter
of interdiction (P11) that the Auditor General is responsible for auditing the 1st
Respondent which clearly, and unequivocally, shows that the 1st Respondent is
a State Agency.
It is to be observed that, in terms of the letter of interdiction (P11), interdiction
of the Petitioner from his employment by the Respondents was based on the
decision made by the Board of Directors at the Board meeting No. 336 held on
29.08.2025 and as per Clause No. 31:1:7 of Chapter XLVIII of the Establishment
Code.
Page 9 of 24
It was specifically resolved in the resolution by the Board of Directors of the 1st
Respondent that decisions pertaining to the disciplinary action are to be made
as per the Establishment Code since it was the procedure which was adhered to
by the 1st Respondent previously, and therefore, it was resolved by the Board of
Directors of the 1st Respondent to withdraw the two labour allowances granted
to the Petitioner and to interdict him immediately, as per Clause No. 31:1:7 of
Chapter XLVIII of the Establishment Code.
Besides, the contract of employment (P2) does not contain any provision with
regard to the interdiction of the Petitioner from his employment.
One of the principal contentions so advanced by the Petitioner in the instant
application is that the Respondents have acted in violation of the provisions of
the Establishment Code (hereinafter called and referred to as the ‘E-Code’)in that
it was contended that the Respondents had failed to adhere to the procedure laid
down in the E-Code when they had proceeded to make the decision to interdict
him without pay as contained in (P11).
It clearly, and unequivocally, manifest from the Board Resolution passed by the
Board of Directors of Elkaduwa Plantations Ltd at the Board Meeting of the
Directors held on 29th August 2025 and the letter of interdiction (P11) that the
procedure laid down in the E-Code relating to the disciplinary action of its
employees had previously, been adhered to and adopted by the 1st Respondent.
It was held by the Supreme Court in Elmore Perera Vs. Major Montagu
Jayawickrema, Minister of Public Administration and Plantation Industries
and Others 1985 [1] SLR 285 at Page 335 that,
“The Establishments Code is the basic document relating to procedures of
disciplinary action against public officers. It has been formulated by the
Cabinet of Ministers under Article 55 (4) of the Constitution in whom such
a power is reposed. This formulation has the characteristics of a policy
decision as it deals with the broad principles and procedures governing
Page 10 of 24
disciplinary action against officers of practically the entire public service
in this country………..The administration of the public service is now an
internal matter of the Executive. It would however appear that the Cabinet,
after due deliberation, has sought to formulate a Code of regulations
containing fair procedures and safeguards balancing the requirements
and interests of the Government with the rights of public officers, and the
legal protection now provided by the law to public officers is contained in
this Code. These procedures are therefore mandatory and cannot be
superseded or disregarded without due legal authority.”
In the light of the facts and the law enumerated above, I would hold that the
interdiction of the Petitioner is governed by the procedure laid down by the E-
Code and therefore, this Court needs to examine whether the Respondents have
violated any statutory provisions contained in the E-Code when the Respondents
had issued the disciplinary order (P11) in the absence of provisions in the
contract of employment (P2) relating to interdiction of the Petitioner from his
employment.
I would therefore, hold that the preliminary legal objection raised by the learned
State Counsel for the Respondents as to the maintainability of the instant
application is not entitled to succeed both in fact and law and as such it should
be over-ruled in-limine.
The only question that remains for our consideration is; Whether the
Respondents had acted in violation of the E-Code when they took the decision to
interdict the Petitioner without pay as contained in the letter dated 08.09.2025
issued by the 2nd Respondent (P11) without any preliminary
investigation/inquiry being held, and/or without offering the Petitioner the
opportunity to provide any explanation/show cause which according to the
Petitioner, is a clear breach of the Establishment Code and rules of Natural
Justice; and if so whether the decision contained in ‘P11’ is arbitrary,
unlawful, malicious, unreasonable, irrational, disproportionate and illegal
Page 11 of 24
and therefore, liable to be quashed by a mandate in the nature of a writ of
certiorari as alleged by the Petitioner [Emphasis is mine]
At this juncture, let me briefly, set out the facts pertaining to the instant
application as averred in the petition by the Petitioner.
The Petitioner who began his career as Assistant-Superintendent of the 1st
Respondent had subsequently, been promoted to the post of Superintendent of
the 1st Respondent; and that he had subsequently, been transferred to the head
office of the 1st Respondent as Marketing Manager of the 1st Respondent with
effect from 01.02.2019 and served in that capacity until he had been interdicted
as per the letter of interdiction (P11) with immediate effect, namely; 08.09.2025
on the allegation that as per the audit conducted during the month of May, 2025
by the Auditor General’s Office, it had been observed that though he was not
allocated with a estate bungalow, he had misused the public funds of Rs.
4,739,947/- as a claim of labour allowances from 01.02.2019 to 28.03.2025;
and that further, 1st Respondent had paid a total of Rs. 610,725/- from
February, 2019 to March, 2025 as EPF to two labourers which the Petitioner had
been claiming allowances for, and therefore, it is not recoverable at any cost to
1st Respondent by which the Petitioner had created a huge loss as a responsible
officer who is working in a government institute.
It is in this view of the matter, let me now, examine the provisions of the E- code
relevant to the issue at hand before us.
Clause 13:1 of the Chapter XLVIII-Volume 2 of the E-Code provides for the
preliminary investigation and it enacts thus; “A preliminary investigation is that
which is conducted by a Disciplinary Authority or Head of Institution or other
Appropriate Authority or by an officer or a Committee of Officers duly authorized
by the above authorities to find facts as are necessary to ascertain the truth of a
suspicion or information that an act of misconduct has been committed by an
officer or several officers, and to find out and report whether there are, prima-
facie, sufficient material and evidence to prefer charges and take disciplinary
Page 12 of 24
action against the officer or officers under suspicion. The primary task of an
officer or a Committee of Officers conducting a preliminary investigation is the
recording of statements of relevant persons, examination of documents and
records, obtaining of originals or certified copies thereof, physical verification of
state-owned assets in the charge of the officer or officers subject to the
investigation, examination of relevant premises, taking over of all articles and
documents which are considered necessary and making their observations and
recommendations on matters found out by them regarding the act of misconduct
committed.”
Clause 31:4 of the Chapter XLVIII-Volume 2 of the E-Code enacts the general
rule that normally a public officer should be interdicted on matters
disclosed in a preliminary investigation held into the charges against him.
On the other hand, Clause 31:5 of the Chapter XLVIII-Volume 2 of the E-Code,
enacts three exceptions to the general rule laid down in Clause 31:4 thereof,
as follows;
31:5 In such instances as given below, a relevant authority may interdict a public
officer even without holding a preliminary investigation as referred to in sub-
section 31:4 above.
31:5:1 Where it is evident to the relevant authority that the continuance of the
officer in service is detrimental to the holding of a preliminary investigation
against him.
31:5:2 Where the first information itself on the suspected acts of misconduct
committed by the officer is sufficient to establish the relevant matters.
31:5:3 Where Court proceedings have been initiated against a public officer in
terms of section 27 of this Code.
Hence, it clearly, appears that the general rule laid down by clause 31:4 of the
Chapter XLVIII-Volume 2 of the E-Code is not in any manner, absolute but it is
always subject to three exceptions laid down by Clauses 31:5:1, 31:5:2 and
Page 13 of 24
31:5:3 thereof, and in such instances, the relevant disciplinary authority may,
interdict the officer concerned without holding a preliminary inquiry however,
subject to the Clause 31:6 thereof.
Clause 31 of the Chapter XLVIII-Volume 2 of the E-Code provides for the
interdiction and compulsory leave wherein it sets out a number of instances
where the relevant Disciplinary Authority, or the relevant Secretary to the
Ministry or Head of Department not holding disciplinary authority, may
forthwith interdict the officer concerned subject to the covering approval of the
Disciplinary Authority., and it enacts thus;
“Where it is disclosed, prima facie, that a public officer has committed either
one or some or all of the following acts of misconduct, the relevant
Disciplinary Authority, or the relevant Secretary to the Ministry or Head of
Department not holding disciplinary authority, may forthwith interdict the
officer concerned subject to the covering approval of the Disciplinary Authority.
However as soon as the letter of interdiction is issued by the Disciplinary
Authority should be informed sending also a copy of such letter to the purpose
of obtaining covering approval.
31:1:1 Non-allegiance to the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of
Sri Lanka.
31:1:2 Act or cause to act in such manner as to bring the Democratic Socialist
Republic of Sri Lanka into disrepute.
31:1:3 Being prosecuted in a Court of Law on anti-government, terrorist or
criminal charges.
31:1:4 Being prosecuted in a Court of Law on bribery or corruption charges.
31:1:5 Being drunk or smelling of liquor within duty hours or within Government
premises.
Page 14 of 24
31:1:6 Use or be in possession of narcotic drugs within duty hours or within
Government premises.
31:1:7 Misappropriate or cause another to misappropriate government
funds.
31:1:8 Misappropriate government resources or cause such misappropriation, or
cause destruction or depreciation of government resources wilfully or
negligently.
31:1:9 Act or cause to act negligently or inadvertently or willfully in such manner
as to harm government interests.
31:1:10 Act in such manner as to bring the public service into disrepute.”
[Emphasis is mine]
Clause 31:6 of the Chapter XLVIII-Volume 2 of the E-Code provides that “Even
in the case of an officer interdicted in terms of 31:5:1 and 31:5:2 above, a
preliminary investigation should be held, without delay, after interdiction.”
What does the word “forthwith” embodied in Clause 31:1 of the Chapter XLVIII-
Volume 2 of the E-Code mean? [Emphasis is mine]
It was held in Queen vs. The Justices of Berkshire [(1878-79) 4 Q.B.D. 469
(471)] that, “The question is substantially one of fact. It is impossible to lay down
any hard and fast rule as to what is the meaning of the word 'immediately', in all
cases. The words 'forthwith' and 'immediately' have the same meaning. They are
stronger than the expression 'within a reasonable time', and imply prompt,
vigorous action, without any delay, and whether there has been such action is
question of fact, having regard to the circumstances of the particular case”.
It was held in Soysa vs. Anglo-Ceylon and General Estates Co. 19 N. L. R.
374 that, “In my opinion the word "forthwith in the enactment in question should
Page 15 of 24
be construed as meaning, not within a period reasonable in the circumstances,
"but "without any delay that can possibly be avoided.”
Hence, it becomes abundantly, clear upon a careful analysis of Clauses 31:5:2
to 31:1 and 31:1:7 of the Chapter XLVIII-Volume 2 of the E-Code altogether that
when a public officer is interdicted before a preliminary investigation being held
not only the requirement in Clause 31:5:1 but also the instances embodied in
Clauses 31:5:2 and 31:5:3 may be considered by the disciplinary authority .
Accordingly, if the disciplinary authority is of the view that the first information
itself on the suspected acts of misconduct alleged to have been committed by the
public officer is sufficient to establish the relevant matters, then such officer can
be forthwith, interdicted even before a preliminary investigation being held
subject to the covering approval of the disciplinary authority in case of the Head
of the Department exercising the said power.
The pivotal contention advanced by the learned Counsel for the Petitioner in
support of the application for notice and the interim orders is that the
Respondents had acted in violation of the E-Code when they took the decision to
interdict the Petitioner without pay as contained in the letter dated 08.09.2025
issued by the 2nd Respondent (P11) without any preliminary
investigation/inquiry being held, and/or without offering the Petitioner the
opportunity to provide any explanation/show cause which according to the
Petitioner, is a clear breach of the Establishment Code and rules of Natural
Justice and hence, the decision contained in ‘P11’ is arbitrary, unlawful,
malicious, unreasonable, irrational, disproportionate and illegal and
therefore, liable to be quashed by a mandate in the nature of a writ of
certiorari as alleged by the Petitioner [Emphasis is mine]
It may now, be examined.
Interdiction of the Petitioner from his employment had derived upon the letter of
interdiction (P11) issued to him by the 2nd Respondent and the material part
Page 16 of 24
thereof, which would be instrumental for the determination of the issue at hand,
may be reproduced verbatim the same as follows;
“We write to inform you that you that as per the recent audit conducted
during the month of May 2025, by the Auditor General's office it has been
observed that though you was not allocated with an estate bungalow, you
have misused the public funds of Rs. 4,739,947 as a claim of Labour
allowances from 01st February 2019 to 28th March 2025.
Further, Elkaduwa Plantations Ltd; (EPL) has paid a total of Rs. 610,725
from February 2019 to March 2025 as EPF to 02 labourers which you have
been claiming allowances for. Therefore, it is not recoverable at any cost
to EPL by which you have created a huge loss as a responsible Officer who
is working in a Government Institute.”
Upon a careful scrutiny of the material part of the letter of interdiction (P11), it
appears that the interdiction of the Petitioner from his employment was based
on an allegation of a serious nature, namely; that though the Petitioner was not
allocated with an estate bungalow, he had misused the public funds of Rs.
4,739,947 as a claim of Labour allowances from 01st February 2019 to 28th
March 2025; and that the 1st Respondent had paid a total of Rs. 610,725 from
February 2019 to March 2025 as EPF to 02 labourers which he had been
claiming allowances for, thus, it was not recoverable at any cost to 1st
Respondent by which he had created a huge loss as a responsible Officer who is
working in a Government Institute.
It is under those circumstances, the Respondent had made a decision to interdict
the Petitioner as per a decision made by the Board of Directors at the Board
meeting no:336 held on 29/08/2025 and as per the Establishment Code Chapter
XLVIII, Clause No: 31:1:7 from the Post of Marketing Manager with immediate
effect without pay, subject to the holding of a disciplinary inquiry on the above
matter immediately. It is this decision of the Respondents contained in (P11) that
Page 17 of 24
the Petitioner now, seeks to impugn before us in the instant application on the
basis as enumerated above.
In terms of the letter of interdiction (P11), the interdiction of the Petitioner from
his employment was solely, and entirely, based on an allegation of misusing
public funds amounting to Rs. 4,739,947/- as a claim of Labour allowances from
01st February 2019 to 28th March 2025 for which he was not entitled by reason
of the fact that he had not been allocated with an estate bungalow for his
occupation by the 1st Respondent; and in consequence, the 1st Respondent had
to pay a non-recoverable total sum of Rs. 610,725/- from February 2019 to
March 2025 as EPF to 02 labourers which he had been claiming allowances for
and by which, the Petitioner had created a huge loss as a responsible Officer who
is working in a Government Institute.
The pertinent question that would then arise for our consideration is; From
where did the Respondents get the first information of the misuse of such a
vast amount of public funds by the Petitioner amounting to Rs. 4,739,947/- for
the purposes of Clause 31:5:2 of Chapter XLVIII of the E-Code to be read with
Clause 31:1 and 31:1:7 thereof?[Emphasis is mine]
The letter of interdiction (P11) is self-explanatory on this pertinent question
wherein, it was stated that it was disclosed as per the recent audit conducted
during the month of May 2025, by the Auditor General's office. This had been
well, supported by the Audit Query directed to the 1st Respondent by the Auditor
General of this Country pursuant to an audit conducted by him on the 1st
Respondent during the period stipulated therein, apparently, by virtue of the
powers vested in him in terms of the provisions of the National Audit Act No. 19
of 2018, and the observations of the Auditor General contained therein would be
pertinent for the resolution of the issue at hand and they may be reproduced
verbatim the same in the language it was made as follows;
Page 18 of 24
“ඇල්කඩුව වැවිලි සමාගමේ ප්‍රධාන කාර්යාලර් ඇතුලුව වතු 10 හි සිදු කරන ලද නිර්ැදි විගණන
පරීක්ෂාමේද පහත සඳහන් කරුණ අනාවරණර් විර්
2.ඇල්කඩුව වැවිලි සමාගමමහි විධාර්ක බංගලා සඳහා අභ්‍යන්තර කේකරුවන් මදමදමනකුමේ

මනාතිබිර්දී, ඔහු විසින් 2019 මපබරවාරි මස සිට 2025 මාය මාසර් දක්වා කේකරුවන්

488,580 ක මසේවක අර්යසාධක අරමුදල් දාර්කත්වර්ක් හා රු. 122,145 ක මසේවක භ්‍ාරකාර
අරමුදල් දාර්කත්වර්ක් දරා තිබූ අතර, එමඟින් රු. 610,725 ක අර්කරගත මනාහැකි අලාභ්‍ර්ක්
සමාගමට දැරීමට සිදුව ඇති බව නිරීක්ෂණර් විර්…..”
There is no reason to reject and/or disbelieve the observations so made by the
Auditor General that the Petitioner had misused the public funds to the value as
enumerated therein for; the observation was based upon an audit conducted by
him by virtue of the powers statutorily, vested in him as enumerated above, and
therefore, the said observations made by the Auditor General should be
construed as the first information on the alleged misuse of public funds by the
Petitioner for all purposes and intents of Clause 31:5:2 of Chapter XLVIII of the
E-Code.
The Observations so made by the Auditor General had been well, supported by
the contents of the letter referred to in paragraph 2(අ)(iv) of the Audit Query,
namely; the letter annexed to his petition of the Petitioner marked as ‘P9’ and
Clause 1 of P9 would be pertinent in this regard and it reads thus;
“You will be entitled for two (02) labour allowances in the usual manner
enjoyed by you presently, based on the audit requirements;” [Emphasis
is mine]
The question that would then, arise is; What does it mean by the phrase “two
(02) labour allowances in the usual manner enjoyed by you presently”?
[Emphasis is mine]
Clauses 8 and 9 of the letter of appointment/contract of employment (P2) would
be important in this regard and they read thus;
තු
Page 20 of 24
“08. QUARTERS : You will be provided with a bungalow free of rent on the
estate supplied with hard and soft furnishings as per Estate Inventory.
09. LABOUR ALLOWANCE: You will be entitled to one indoor and one
outdoor labourer. Such labourers shall be remunerated by the Elkaduwa
Plantations Limited through the Estate Checkroll.”
It would also be pertinent to examine Clause 1 of ‘P7’ which is the letter of
transfer dated 03.01.2019 by which the Petitioner was transferred to the head
office as the Marketing Manager and it reads thus;
“1) Labour allowances :-Since your working place is Head Office you will
not be entitled for an Official Bungalow and you will not be paid the normal
labour allowances.”
A careful reading of Clauses 8 and 9 of the letter of appointment/contract of
employment (P2) in conjunction with Clause 1 of ‘P7’, it becomes abundantly,
clear that the Petitioner is entitled to two labour allowances only where he is
provided with an official bungalow; and that he is not entitled to claim the two
labour allowances in cash nor is he entitled to claim labour allowances for his
private residence. It is this sense that the phrase “two (02) labour allowances in
the usual manner enjoyed by you presently” contained in ‘P9’ should be
construed and understood as observed by the Auditor General in the Audit Query
directed to the 1st Respondent as enumerated above. However, the Petitioner had
nevertheless, claimed two labour allowances in Cash for the period stipulated in
the letter of interdiction (P11) as enumerated above, notwithstanding the fact
that he had not been provided with an official bungalow for the said period.
[Emphasis is mine]
Hence, it prima facie appears that the first information itself namely; Audit Query
directed by the Auditor General to the 1st Respondent, on the suspected acts of
Page 21 of 24
misconduct alleged to have been committed by the Petitioner is sufficient to
establish the relevant matters.
Clause 31:11 and 31:11:2 of Chapter XLVIII of the E-Code enact thus;
“31:11 A public officer interdicted under the following circumstances
should not be paid any emoluments during the period of interdiction.
31:11:2 Where misappropriation of a serious nature of public funds and
property is committed or where they are caused to be destroyed or
depreciated by acts of commission or omission.”
Hence, the Respondents are entitled to interdict the Petitioner without pay acting
under the powers vested in them by Clause 31:5 Chapter XLVIII of the E-Code
to be read with Clauses 31:5:2, 31:1, 31:1:7, 31:11 and 31:11:2 thereof, and
therefore, the decision of the Respondents contained in ‘P11’ to interdict the
Petitioner without pay can sustain in law.
Besides, it clearly, appears from a careful reading of the resolution adopted by
the Board of Directors of the 1st Respondent that the Board of Directors had
proceeded to interdict the Petitioner without pay taking into consideration the
Audit Report as the preliminary investigation for all purposes and intents of the
said provisions of the E-Code.
Hence, it clearly, appears that the Respondents had interdicted the Petitioner
without pay on the alleged misuse of public funds to the value as enumerated
above, by virtue of the powers vested in them by Clauses 31:1, 31:1:7, 31:5 and
31:5:2 of Chapter XLVIII of the E-Code thereby, making the decision of the
Respondents to interdict the Petitioner without pay, intra-vires.
It is in this view of the matter, let me now, consider the principal ground upon
which the Petitioner seeks to impugn the decision contained in ‘P11’.
Page 22 of 24
The common law grounds of judicial review are illegality, irrationality and
procedural impropriety as held in Council of Civil Service Union v. Minister
for the Civil Service (1985) AC 374(HL). There, Lord Diplock went on to state
as follows;
"By "illegality" as a ground for judicial review I mean that the decision-maker
must understand correctly the law that regulates his decision-making power and
must give effect to it. Whether he has or not is par excellence a justiciable
question to be decided, in the event of dispute, by those persons, the judges, by
whom the judicial power of the state is exercisable.
In the instant application, the Respondents had having clearly, understood the
law that regulates its decision contained in ‘P11’ and given effect to it as
enumerated above. Hence, the decision contained in ‘P11’ is not in any manner,
illegal and therefore, intra-vires.
Lord Diplock in the case referred to above went on to state as follows;
“By "irrationality" I mean what can by now be succinctly referred to as
"Wednesbury unreasonableness" (Associated Provincial Picture Houses
Ltd. v. Wednesbury Corporation [1948] 1 KB 223). It applies to a decision
which is so outrageous in its defiance of logic or of accepted moral
standards that no sensible person who had applied his mind to the
question to be decided could have arrived at it. Whether a decision falls
within this category is a question that judges by their training and
experience should be well equipped to answer, or else there would be
something badly wrong with our judicial system. To justify the court's
exercise of this role, resort I think is today no longer needed to Viscount
Radcliffe's ingenious explanation in Edwards v. Bairstow [1956] AC 14 of
irrationality as a ground for a court's reversal of a decision by ascribing it
to an inferred though unidentifiable mistake of law by the decision-maker.
"Irrationality" by now can stand upon its own feet as an accepted ground
on which a decision may be attacked by judicial review.
Page 23 of 24
For the reasons set out above, the decision contained in ‘P11’ cannot in any
manner, be said to be irrational for; it is not so outrageous in its defiance of logic
nor of accepted moral standards that no sensible person who had applied his
mind to the question to be decided could have arrived at it.
Lord Diplock in the case referred to above, went on to state further as follows;
“I have described the third head as "procedural impropriety" rather than
failure to observe basic rules of natural justice or failure to act with
procedural fairness towards the person who will be affected by the
decision. This is because susceptibility to judicial review under this head
covers also failure by an administrative tribunal to observe procedural
rules that are expressly laid down in the legislative instrument by which
its jurisdiction is conferred, even where such failure does not involve any
denial of natural justice. But the instant case is not concerned with the
proceedings of an administrative tribunal at all.”
In view of the foregoing reasons, the decision of the Respondents to interdict the
Petitioner as contained in ‘P11’, cannot successfully, be attacked on the basis of
procedural impropriety for; the said decision had been arrived at by the
Respondents by adhering to the procedural rules laid down in the E-Code as
morefully, enumerated above.
Besides, it is to be observed, that the Petitioner still has ample opportunity to
challenge any decision of the Respondents after the investigations (including the
domestic inquiry) are over. The interdiction will not be a presumption of guilt
and it cannot be considered as a punishment. The interdiction is one of the
foremost processes used to initiate investigations in to allege misconducts.
For the reasons enumerated above, I would find myself unable to agree with the
contention advanced by the learned Counsel for the Petitioner that the decision
contained in ‘P11’, is arbitrary and/or unlawful and/or malicious and/or
irrational and/or disproportionate and/or illegal.
Page 24 of 24
In the light of the law and the facts enumerated above, I would hold that the
Petitioner has not made out a prima facie case for notice.
I would therefore, refuse to issue notices on the Respondents.
In the result, I would dismiss the instant application, however, without costs.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
D. THOTAWATTA, J.
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
